While the CMA closed its inquiry after developers agreed to contribute £100m towards affordable housing schemes and signed commitments to avoid future information sharing, the new legal claim suggests the alleged practices may have affected prices as far back as 2015. Homeowners involved in the case could potentially receive between £3,100 and £6,200 each in compensation if the claim succeeds.

What this means for homeowners

For those who purchased new-build properties during the specified period, this development could be significant. The claim argues that reduced competition between developers may have resulted in higher prices than would otherwise have been the case.

Affected homeowners might wish to:

  • Monitor updates about the case’s progress through official channels
  • Consider whether they fall within the claimant group (those who bought eligible properties between 2015-2026)
  • Review their property purchase documentation

Legal representatives involved in the case have emphasised its importance for ensuring fair competition in the housing market. Patrick Teague of Geradin Partners noted the claim raises crucial questions about market practices, while McLaren stressed the need to protect consumers making one of their most substantial financial commitments.

As the case develops, homeowners awaiting potential compensation should be aware that tribunal processes can take considerable time. Those with concerns may benefit from seeking independent legal advice to understand their position.

item.title: What the average asking price buys across Great Britain
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The UK’s property market continues to showcase stark regional differences, with the national average asking price of £378,000 offering dramatically varied purchasing power across the country. According to recent analysis by Rightmove, this budget can secure anything from a spacious family home to a compact studio flat, depending on the location.

Regional Variations in Property Prices

In regions such as Scotland, the North of England, and the Midlands, buyers with the average budget can often find themselves purchasing four or five-bedroom properties. For example, North Lanarkshire offers five-bedroom detached homes at an average asking price of £376,052, while similar properties in areas like Charnwood, County Durham, Liverpool, and Kirklees are also available for less than the national average. Families seeking more space might find these regions particularly appealing, as their budgets stretch significantly further compared to other parts of the UK.

In contrast, London and parts of the South East present a very different picture. Here, the same budget is more likely to secure a studio or one-bedroom flat. Tower Hamlets, for instance, has studio flats averaging £357,556, whilst Hackney offers similar properties at £341,021. In Richmond upon Thames and Haringey, one-bedroom flats average £362,322 and £345,677 respectively. These figures highlight the challenges faced by buyers in the capital, where affordability remains a pressing issue.

Buyer Behaviour in the Current Market

Rightmove’s analysis suggests that affordability is a key driver of buyer behaviour, particularly in a market where the number of homes for sale is historically high for this time of year. Colleen Babcock, Rightmove’s property commentator, noted that buyers are increasingly scrutinising value for money. “This research demonstrates just how much variation there can be in what the same budget buys across different parts of Britain,” she said. “For some, the average budget might mean a flat in a more expensive city, while in other areas, it could stretch to a much larger family home.”

With affordability remaining a priority, many buyers are taking their time to compare what they can secure within their budget before making a decision. Homes that are well-priced for their size, location, and condition are more likely to attract attention in the current climate.

What £378,000 Buys Across the UK

  • East Midlands: 4-bed semi-detached (£335,423)
  • East of England: 3-bed semi-detached (£377,448)
  • London: 1-bed flat (£369,305)
  • North East: 4-bed detached (£369,075)
  • North West: 4-bed semi-detached (£377,202)
  • Scotland: 4-bed detached (£376,893)
  • South East: 3-bed flat (£375,917)
  • South West: 3-bed flat (£348,836)
  • Wales: 4-bed semi-detached (£341,098)
  • West Midlands: 4-bed semi-detached (£376,768)
  • Yorkshire and The Humber: 4-bed semi-detached (£347,104)

These regional disparities underscore the importance of considering location when planning a property purchase. Buyers might wish to weigh their priorities carefully, balancing space, location, and affordability to find the right home for their needs.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The UK’s property market continues to showcase stark regional differences, with the national average asking price of £378,000 offering dramatically varied purchasing power across the country. According to recent analysis by Rightmove, this budget can secure anything from a spacious family home to a compact studio flat, depending on the location.

Regional Variations in Property Prices

In regions such as Scotland, the North of England, and the Midlands, buyers with the average budget can often find themselves purchasing four or five-bedroom properties. For example, North Lanarkshire offers five-bedroom detached homes at an average asking price of £376,052, while similar properties in areas like Charnwood, County Durham, Liverpool, and Kirklees are also available for less than the national average. Families seeking more space might find these regions particularly appealing, as their budgets stretch significantly further compared to other parts of the UK.

In contrast, London and parts of the South East present a very different picture. Here, the same budget is more likely to secure a studio or one-bedroom flat. Tower Hamlets, for instance, has studio flats averaging £357,556, whilst Hackney offers similar properties at £341,021. In Richmond upon Thames and Haringey, one-bedroom flats average £362,322 and £345,677 respectively. These figures highlight the challenges faced by buyers in the capital, where affordability remains a pressing issue.

Buyer Behaviour in the Current Market

Rightmove’s analysis suggests that affordability is a key driver of buyer behaviour, particularly in a market where the number of homes for sale is historically high for this time of year. Colleen Babcock, Rightmove’s property commentator, noted that buyers are increasingly scrutinising value for money. “This research demonstrates just how much variation there can be in what the same budget buys across different parts of Britain,” she said. “For some, the average budget might mean a flat in a more expensive city, while in other areas, it could stretch to a much larger family home.”

With affordability remaining a priority, many buyers are taking their time to compare what they can secure within their budget before making a decision. Homes that are well-priced for their size, location, and condition are more likely to attract attention in the current climate.

What £378,000 Buys Across the UK

  • East Midlands: 4-bed semi-detached (£335,423)
  • East of England: 3-bed semi-detached (£377,448)
  • London: 1-bed flat (£369,305)
  • North East: 4-bed detached (£369,075)
  • North West: 4-bed semi-detached (£377,202)
  • Scotland: 4-bed detached (£376,893)
  • South East: 3-bed flat (£375,917)
  • South West: 3-bed flat (£348,836)
  • Wales: 4-bed semi-detached (£341,098)
  • West Midlands: 4-bed semi-detached (£376,768)
  • Yorkshire and The Humber: 4-bed semi-detached (£347,104)

These regional disparities underscore the importance of considering location when planning a property purchase. Buyers might wish to weigh their priorities carefully, balancing space, location, and affordability to find the right home for their needs.

item.title: Property Industry unites for charity walk in memory of Sarah Edmundson
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The UK property sector is coming together this autumn for a special charity walk along the Thames Path, honouring industry leader Sarah Edmundson while raising funds for breast cancer research. The event, organised by Women in Estate Agency (WiEA) and Agents Together, invites estate agents, letting agents and property professionals to join the Thames Path Ultra Challenge on 12th September 2026.

Walking for a cause

Participants can choose between a 25km or 50km route along the scenic Thames Path, starting at Putney Bridge and passing through Richmond, Hampton Court and Windsor. All funds raised will go to Breast Cancer Now, supporting vital research and services for those affected by the disease. The walk serves as both a tribute to Sarah Edmundson, a respected figure in the industry, and an opportunity for the property community to unite for an important cause.

Sarah, who passed away in 2023, was the former CEO of Agents Together and a founding board member of WiEA. Known for her dedication to supporting professionals throughout their careers, her legacy continues to inspire colleagues across the sector. The joint team, named WiEA Together, reflects the collaborative spirit Sarah championed throughout her career.

Industry-wide participation

Organisers emphasise that the event welcomes everyone in the property industry, whether they knew Sarah personally, have been affected by breast cancer, or simply want to contribute to a meaningful cause. Vicky Bibiris, chair of ambassadors at Agents Together, noted: "Sarah believed in creating spaces where estate agents could feel supported. Walking alongside Women in Estate Agency feels fitting as both communities share that same purpose."

Danielle Nash from WiEA added: "This walk represents community in action - not just talking about support, but showing it. Sarah helped shape WiEA's spirit of togetherness, and this event allows the industry to honour her memory while supporting a cause that touches so many lives."

With breast cancer affecting one in seven UK women during their lifetime, the organisers stress the personal connection many in the property sector have with the cause. The walk provides an opportunity for professionals to come together, enjoy the outdoors, and make a tangible difference while remembering an influential industry figure.

Those interested in participating or supporting the event can find more details through the WiEA and Agents Together websites, with registration now open for individuals and teams across the property industry.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The UK property sector is coming together this autumn for a special charity walk along the Thames Path, honouring industry leader Sarah Edmundson while raising funds for breast cancer research. The event, organised by Women in Estate Agency (WiEA) and Agents Together, invites estate agents, letting agents and property professionals to join the Thames Path Ultra Challenge on 12th September 2026.

Walking for a cause

Participants can choose between a 25km or 50km route along the scenic Thames Path, starting at Putney Bridge and passing through Richmond, Hampton Court and Windsor. All funds raised will go to Breast Cancer Now, supporting vital research and services for those affected by the disease. The walk serves as both a tribute to Sarah Edmundson, a respected figure in the industry, and an opportunity for the property community to unite for an important cause.

Sarah, who passed away in 2023, was the former CEO of Agents Together and a founding board member of WiEA. Known for her dedication to supporting professionals throughout their careers, her legacy continues to inspire colleagues across the sector. The joint team, named WiEA Together, reflects the collaborative spirit Sarah championed throughout her career.

Industry-wide participation

Organisers emphasise that the event welcomes everyone in the property industry, whether they knew Sarah personally, have been affected by breast cancer, or simply want to contribute to a meaningful cause. Vicky Bibiris, chair of ambassadors at Agents Together, noted: "Sarah believed in creating spaces where estate agents could feel supported. Walking alongside Women in Estate Agency feels fitting as both communities share that same purpose."

Danielle Nash from WiEA added: "This walk represents community in action - not just talking about support, but showing it. Sarah helped shape WiEA's spirit of togetherness, and this event allows the industry to honour her memory while supporting a cause that touches so many lives."

With breast cancer affecting one in seven UK women during their lifetime, the organisers stress the personal connection many in the property sector have with the cause. The walk provides an opportunity for professionals to come together, enjoy the outdoors, and make a tangible difference while remembering an influential industry figure.

Those interested in participating or supporting the event can find more details through the WiEA and Agents Together websites, with registration now open for individuals and teams across the property industry.

item.title: Andy Burnham’s housing plans could ‘utterly transform’ Britain
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Greater Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham has proposed ambitious housing reforms that could reshape Britain’s property landscape, including plans for the largest council housebuilding drive since the 1940s. The proposals form part of his wider "No 10 North" initiative aimed at decentralising government decision-making from Westminster to regional hubs across England.

A New Era for Social Housing?

Burnham’s blueprint highlights the dramatic decline in council housing stock, with 1.5 million social homes lost since the 1980s – coinciding with current housing waiting lists of similar magnitude. His solution involves maximising underused public land and prioritising high-density developments in urban areas to reduce construction costs.

Housing charities have welcomed the proposals, with Shelter’s chief executive describing the potential impact as transformative. The organisation notes that properly funded council-led developments could restore the social housing safety net that once helped millions of families establish stable lives. However, success would require significant policy changes, including debt relief for local authorities and substantial capital investment.

Regional Rebalancing Through Housing

The "No 10 North" concept extends beyond housing, forming part of a broader strategy to redistribute economic growth across England’s regions. Alongside the council housebuilding programme, Burnham’s vision includes urban regeneration projects, industrial renewal and public utility reforms.

Economists suggest such regional rebalancing could help address longstanding housing affordability issues outside London and the South East. By concentrating development in existing urban centres, the plans might also reduce pressure on greenfield sites while improving infrastructure efficiency.

As political debates around housing intensify, Burnham’s proposals have reignited discussions about the role of local authorities in solving Britain’s housing crisis. With waiting lists at record levels and affordability stretched, the coming months may see increased focus on alternative solutions to the nation’s property challenges.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Greater Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham has proposed ambitious housing reforms that could reshape Britain’s property landscape, including plans for the largest council housebuilding drive since the 1940s. The proposals form part of his wider "No 10 North" initiative aimed at decentralising government decision-making from Westminster to regional hubs across England.

A New Era for Social Housing?

Burnham’s blueprint highlights the dramatic decline in council housing stock, with 1.5 million social homes lost since the 1980s – coinciding with current housing waiting lists of similar magnitude. His solution involves maximising underused public land and prioritising high-density developments in urban areas to reduce construction costs.

Housing charities have welcomed the proposals, with Shelter’s chief executive describing the potential impact as transformative. The organisation notes that properly funded council-led developments could restore the social housing safety net that once helped millions of families establish stable lives. However, success would require significant policy changes, including debt relief for local authorities and substantial capital investment.

Regional Rebalancing Through Housing

The "No 10 North" concept extends beyond housing, forming part of a broader strategy to redistribute economic growth across England’s regions. Alongside the council housebuilding programme, Burnham’s vision includes urban regeneration projects, industrial renewal and public utility reforms.

Economists suggest such regional rebalancing could help address longstanding housing affordability issues outside London and the South East. By concentrating development in existing urban centres, the plans might also reduce pressure on greenfield sites while improving infrastructure efficiency.

As political debates around housing intensify, Burnham’s proposals have reignited discussions about the role of local authorities in solving Britain’s housing crisis. With waiting lists at record levels and affordability stretched, the coming months may see increased focus on alternative solutions to the nation’s property challenges.

item.title: Property industry reacts to Zoopla House Price Index
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The UK housing market continues to show signs of cooling, with Zoopla's latest House Price Index revealing weaker buyer demand and slower sales compared to last year. Three in five properties listed since January remain unsold, while agreed sales over the past four weeks have fallen 7% annually. House price growth has slowed to just 1.4%, with London seeing declines while northern regions maintain modest growth.

Market challenges persist

Industry experts point to multiple factors dampening market activity. Higher mortgage rates, political uncertainty ahead of the general election, and global economic concerns appear to be weighing on buyer confidence. Estate agents report properties taking longer to sell, though completed transactions generally proceed without significant price renegotiations.

The market faces particular challenges from:

  • Elevated mortgage rates making affordability tougher
  • Political uncertainty affecting both domestic policy and taxation
  • An oversupply of properties in some price brackets
  • Buyers adopting a 'wait-and-see' approach

Industry perspectives on market conditions

Property professionals highlight how current conditions differ from last year's market. Jeremy Leaf, a north London estate agent, notes that while most agreed sales progress, the process has become slower. He suggests this more cautious environment may represent the "new normal" for the foreseeable future.

Tom Bill of Knight Frank warns that speculation around potential tax changes could further suppress demand, particularly if proposals lack credibility or immediate feasibility. With the spring bounce in activity cut short this year, the traditional summer slowdown may arrive earlier than usual.

Buying agents like Nigel Bishop of Recoco Property Search observe that many prospective purchasers are choosing to delay decisions until either interest rates fall or the economic outlook becomes clearer. This cautious approach particularly affects discretionary movers who aren't under pressure to transact.

While current conditions present challenges, the fundamental reasons for moving - job changes, family circumstances or retirement - continue to drive activity for those who need to transact. For homeowners considering their next steps, obtaining professional mortgage advice and carefully researching local market conditions could help navigate this complex environment.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The UK housing market continues to show signs of cooling, with Zoopla's latest House Price Index revealing weaker buyer demand and slower sales compared to last year. Three in five properties listed since January remain unsold, while agreed sales over the past four weeks have fallen 7% annually. House price growth has slowed to just 1.4%, with London seeing declines while northern regions maintain modest growth.

Market challenges persist

Industry experts point to multiple factors dampening market activity. Higher mortgage rates, political uncertainty ahead of the general election, and global economic concerns appear to be weighing on buyer confidence. Estate agents report properties taking longer to sell, though completed transactions generally proceed without significant price renegotiations.

The market faces particular challenges from:

  • Elevated mortgage rates making affordability tougher
  • Political uncertainty affecting both domestic policy and taxation
  • An oversupply of properties in some price brackets
  • Buyers adopting a 'wait-and-see' approach

Industry perspectives on market conditions

Property professionals highlight how current conditions differ from last year's market. Jeremy Leaf, a north London estate agent, notes that while most agreed sales progress, the process has become slower. He suggests this more cautious environment may represent the "new normal" for the foreseeable future.

Tom Bill of Knight Frank warns that speculation around potential tax changes could further suppress demand, particularly if proposals lack credibility or immediate feasibility. With the spring bounce in activity cut short this year, the traditional summer slowdown may arrive earlier than usual.

Buying agents like Nigel Bishop of Recoco Property Search observe that many prospective purchasers are choosing to delay decisions until either interest rates fall or the economic outlook becomes clearer. This cautious approach particularly affects discretionary movers who aren't under pressure to transact.

While current conditions present challenges, the fundamental reasons for moving - job changes, family circumstances or retirement - continue to drive activity for those who need to transact. For homeowners considering their next steps, obtaining professional mortgage advice and carefully researching local market conditions could help navigate this complex environment.

item.title: Can devolution deliver more homes?
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The debate around housing policy and devolution has intensified as regional mayors push for greater control over planning decisions. Andy Burnham’s proposal for a "circuit breaker" to shift powers from central government to local authorities raises important questions about how England’s housing crisis might be addressed under a more devolved system.

The tension between local and national housing targets

For years, successive governments have taken a stronger role in setting housing targets, often due to concerns that local authorities alone may not plan for enough homes. Policies like the Standard Method and Housing Delivery Test reflect this centralised approach, driven by the recognition that housing demand frequently crosses council boundaries. Local opposition to development can complicate matters, as existing residents—who vote—often resist new housing, while future occupants have no say in the process.

Burnham’s vision of greater devolution challenges this dynamic. If regional mayors gain control over housing, skills, and infrastructure, who then determines how many homes are needed? If Whitehall continues to dictate targets, critics may argue it undermines true devolution. Yet if central government steps back entirely, regional leaders could face the same political pressures that have historically constrained local councils.

A potential middle ground

One possible solution could involve a hybrid approach, where national government sets housing targets on a regional basis, while local leaders decide where those homes should be built. Combined authorities, working with councils, could distribute housing requirements across their areas, ensuring each locality contributes fairly to meeting demand. This model might balance accountability with flexibility, allowing regional mayors to align housing with transport and economic strategies.

Such an arrangement could address two key challenges: the need for a coordinated, large-scale response to housing shortages, and the desire for local decision-making. It may also help overcome the "not in my backyard" mindset by spreading development more evenly, rather than concentrating it in areas with less resistance.

The discussion around devolution and housing highlights the complexities of planning policy. While greater local control has merits, a purely decentralised system risks repeating past struggles. Striking the right balance between regional autonomy and national oversight could be crucial in tackling England’s persistent housing shortage.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The debate around housing policy and devolution has intensified as regional mayors push for greater control over planning decisions. Andy Burnham’s proposal for a "circuit breaker" to shift powers from central government to local authorities raises important questions about how England’s housing crisis might be addressed under a more devolved system.

The tension between local and national housing targets

For years, successive governments have taken a stronger role in setting housing targets, often due to concerns that local authorities alone may not plan for enough homes. Policies like the Standard Method and Housing Delivery Test reflect this centralised approach, driven by the recognition that housing demand frequently crosses council boundaries. Local opposition to development can complicate matters, as existing residents—who vote—often resist new housing, while future occupants have no say in the process.

Burnham’s vision of greater devolution challenges this dynamic. If regional mayors gain control over housing, skills, and infrastructure, who then determines how many homes are needed? If Whitehall continues to dictate targets, critics may argue it undermines true devolution. Yet if central government steps back entirely, regional leaders could face the same political pressures that have historically constrained local councils.

A potential middle ground

One possible solution could involve a hybrid approach, where national government sets housing targets on a regional basis, while local leaders decide where those homes should be built. Combined authorities, working with councils, could distribute housing requirements across their areas, ensuring each locality contributes fairly to meeting demand. This model might balance accountability with flexibility, allowing regional mayors to align housing with transport and economic strategies.

Such an arrangement could address two key challenges: the need for a coordinated, large-scale response to housing shortages, and the desire for local decision-making. It may also help overcome the "not in my backyard" mindset by spreading development more evenly, rather than concentrating it in areas with less resistance.

The discussion around devolution and housing highlights the complexities of planning policy. While greater local control has merits, a purely decentralised system risks repeating past struggles. Striking the right balance between regional autonomy and national oversight could be crucial in tackling England’s persistent housing shortage.

item.title: Housing market stalls as buyer demand drops sharply
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The UK housing market is showing signs of slowing, with buyer demand dropping by 15% compared to last year and nearly 60% of properties listed since January remaining unsold, according to Zoopla’s latest House Price Index. Higher mortgage rates and political uncertainty appear to be key factors dampening buyer confidence, with agreed sales down 7% nationally over the past four weeks.

Regional variations in market slowdown

The impact of tighter market conditions varies significantly across the country. Wales has experienced the sharpest decline in agreed sales at 12% below last year’s levels, followed by the East Midlands (11%), East of England (10%) and South West (10%). London has seen a 9% reduction in sales, while the West Midlands has recorded the most notable drop in buyer demand – down 30% year-on-year.

Flats are proving particularly difficult to sell, with over two-thirds of one and two-bedroom apartments listed this year still on the market. First-time buyers appear most affected by higher borrowing costs and stamp duty, especially in London where the average monthly mortgage payment has risen by £232 compared to just £66 in the North East.

House price growth slows across most regions

The cooling market is beginning to influence property prices, with UK annual growth slowing to 1.4%. Regional disparities remain pronounced – the North East and North West lead with 3.5% growth, while Scotland follows closely at 3%. In contrast, London prices have fallen for nine consecutive months (-0.2%), with the South East also recording a slight decline (-0.3%).

Zoopla suggests that realistic asking prices will become increasingly important for sellers as the market adjusts. Richard Donnell, Zoopla’s executive director, notes: “While higher mortgage rates have impacted affordability and sales, the situation remains less severe than during the 2022 mini-budget period. Northern England and Scotland continue to see steady buyer activity, demonstrating the market isn’t moving uniformly.”

With mortgage rates beginning to ease from their April peak of around 5%, prospective buyers might find improving conditions in coming months. However, the current climate suggests sellers may need to adjust expectations to attract increasingly cautious purchasers.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The UK housing market is showing signs of slowing, with buyer demand dropping by 15% compared to last year and nearly 60% of properties listed since January remaining unsold, according to Zoopla’s latest House Price Index. Higher mortgage rates and political uncertainty appear to be key factors dampening buyer confidence, with agreed sales down 7% nationally over the past four weeks.

Regional variations in market slowdown

The impact of tighter market conditions varies significantly across the country. Wales has experienced the sharpest decline in agreed sales at 12% below last year’s levels, followed by the East Midlands (11%), East of England (10%) and South West (10%). London has seen a 9% reduction in sales, while the West Midlands has recorded the most notable drop in buyer demand – down 30% year-on-year.

Flats are proving particularly difficult to sell, with over two-thirds of one and two-bedroom apartments listed this year still on the market. First-time buyers appear most affected by higher borrowing costs and stamp duty, especially in London where the average monthly mortgage payment has risen by £232 compared to just £66 in the North East.

House price growth slows across most regions

The cooling market is beginning to influence property prices, with UK annual growth slowing to 1.4%. Regional disparities remain pronounced – the North East and North West lead with 3.5% growth, while Scotland follows closely at 3%. In contrast, London prices have fallen for nine consecutive months (-0.2%), with the South East also recording a slight decline (-0.3%).

Zoopla suggests that realistic asking prices will become increasingly important for sellers as the market adjusts. Richard Donnell, Zoopla’s executive director, notes: “While higher mortgage rates have impacted affordability and sales, the situation remains less severe than during the 2022 mini-budget period. Northern England and Scotland continue to see steady buyer activity, demonstrating the market isn’t moving uniformly.”

With mortgage rates beginning to ease from their April peak of around 5%, prospective buyers might find improving conditions in coming months. However, the current climate suggests sellers may need to adjust expectations to attract increasingly cautious purchasers.

item.title: Mortgage market cools as borrowing and approvals slide
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK mortgage market showed signs of cooling in May, with both borrowing levels and home purchase approvals declining according to Bank of England figures. Net mortgage lending dropped to £2.9bn - the lowest monthly figure since May 2025 and a significant fall from April's £4.4bn. Meanwhile, approvals for house purchases fell to 56,200, below the six-month average of 63,300.

Market slowdown extends to remortgaging

The softening demand wasn't limited to home purchases. Remortgage approvals saw an even steeper decline, plummeting from 51,200 in April to 33,300 in May. This contrasts with relatively stable consumer credit borrowing, which remained at £1.7bn overall. Within this figure, credit card borrowing eased to £0.6bn while personal loans and car finance borrowing rose to £1.1bn.

Lucian Cook of Savills noted that May's figures provide "something of a reality check" after unexpectedly strong mortgage approval numbers earlier in the year. He pointed to recent improvements in mortgage market competition and easing fixed rates as factors that could help mitigate affordability pressures for buyers.

Mixed outlook for housing market

Industry experts offered differing perspectives on what the figures mean for the housing market's trajectory. Savills forecasts a 2% decline in mainstream house prices this year, with growth expected to return in 2027. Cook suggested that while recent mortgage rate improvements might limit price falls, the broader economic outlook points to subdued market conditions for the rest of 2026.

However, Yopa CEO Verona Frankish struck a more optimistic note, describing the approval dip as part of normal market fluctuations rather than a sign of weakening fundamentals. She highlighted strengthening demand throughout 2026, driven by competitive mortgage rates and growing market stability. Frankish observed that buyers appear increasingly willing to proceed with purchases rather than waiting for potential further rate cuts.

With lenders continuing to compete for business and expectations of monetary policy easing still in play, many industry figures remain cautiously positive about buyer activity in the coming months. While monthly approval figures may continue to fluctuate, the underlying market appears to be maintaining its resilience.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK mortgage market showed signs of cooling in May, with both borrowing levels and home purchase approvals declining according to Bank of England figures. Net mortgage lending dropped to £2.9bn - the lowest monthly figure since May 2025 and a significant fall from April's £4.4bn. Meanwhile, approvals for house purchases fell to 56,200, below the six-month average of 63,300.

Market slowdown extends to remortgaging

The softening demand wasn't limited to home purchases. Remortgage approvals saw an even steeper decline, plummeting from 51,200 in April to 33,300 in May. This contrasts with relatively stable consumer credit borrowing, which remained at £1.7bn overall. Within this figure, credit card borrowing eased to £0.6bn while personal loans and car finance borrowing rose to £1.1bn.

Lucian Cook of Savills noted that May's figures provide "something of a reality check" after unexpectedly strong mortgage approval numbers earlier in the year. He pointed to recent improvements in mortgage market competition and easing fixed rates as factors that could help mitigate affordability pressures for buyers.

Mixed outlook for housing market

Industry experts offered differing perspectives on what the figures mean for the housing market's trajectory. Savills forecasts a 2% decline in mainstream house prices this year, with growth expected to return in 2027. Cook suggested that while recent mortgage rate improvements might limit price falls, the broader economic outlook points to subdued market conditions for the rest of 2026.

However, Yopa CEO Verona Frankish struck a more optimistic note, describing the approval dip as part of normal market fluctuations rather than a sign of weakening fundamentals. She highlighted strengthening demand throughout 2026, driven by competitive mortgage rates and growing market stability. Frankish observed that buyers appear increasingly willing to proceed with purchases rather than waiting for potential further rate cuts.

With lenders continuing to compete for business and expectations of monetary policy easing still in play, many industry figures remain cautiously positive about buyer activity in the coming months. While monthly approval figures may continue to fluctuate, the underlying market appears to be maintaining its resilience.

item.title: Buy-to-let investment demand collapses across the UK
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The buy-to-let market in the UK has seen a significant downturn in demand, with landlords reassessing their property investment strategies amid changing economic conditions. Recent data suggests a marked decline in buy-to-let purchases across major cities, as rising mortgage costs and regulatory changes reshape the rental property landscape.

Why Are Landlords Retreating From Buy-To-Let?

Several factors appear to be contributing to the cooling of buy-to-let investment activity:

  • Higher mortgage interest rates increasing borrowing costs
  • Recent tax changes reducing profitability for landlords
  • Growing regulatory requirements adding to administrative burdens
  • Economic uncertainty making long-term investments less attractive

Smaller portfolio landlords in particular might be reconsidering their positions, with some potentially exiting the market altogether. This trend could lead to a shift in the rental property supply across many UK regions.

What Does This Mean For The Property Market?

The reduction in buy-to-let investment could have several implications for the wider housing market:

  • Potential decrease in competition for certain property types
  • Possible stabilisation or reduction in house prices in some areas
  • Changes in rental supply that may affect tenant options
  • Opportunities for first-time buyers in previously landlord-dominated markets

Prospective investors might wish to carefully evaluate the current climate, considering both the challenges and potential opportunities that this market shift presents. Those with existing portfolios may want to review their strategies in light of the changing conditions.

While the buy-to-let sector faces headwinds, the long-term fundamentals of property investment remain. Market conditions continue to evolve, and landlords who adapt their approaches may still find viable opportunities in the current environment.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The buy-to-let market in the UK has seen a significant downturn in demand, with landlords reassessing their property investment strategies amid changing economic conditions. Recent data suggests a marked decline in buy-to-let purchases across major cities, as rising mortgage costs and regulatory changes reshape the rental property landscape.

Why Are Landlords Retreating From Buy-To-Let?

Several factors appear to be contributing to the cooling of buy-to-let investment activity:

  • Higher mortgage interest rates increasing borrowing costs
  • Recent tax changes reducing profitability for landlords
  • Growing regulatory requirements adding to administrative burdens
  • Economic uncertainty making long-term investments less attractive

Smaller portfolio landlords in particular might be reconsidering their positions, with some potentially exiting the market altogether. This trend could lead to a shift in the rental property supply across many UK regions.

What Does This Mean For The Property Market?

The reduction in buy-to-let investment could have several implications for the wider housing market:

  • Potential decrease in competition for certain property types
  • Possible stabilisation or reduction in house prices in some areas
  • Changes in rental supply that may affect tenant options
  • Opportunities for first-time buyers in previously landlord-dominated markets

Prospective investors might wish to carefully evaluate the current climate, considering both the challenges and potential opportunities that this market shift presents. Those with existing portfolios may want to review their strategies in light of the changing conditions.

While the buy-to-let sector faces headwinds, the long-term fundamentals of property investment remain. Market conditions continue to evolve, and landlords who adapt their approaches may still find viable opportunities in the current environment.

item.title: More mortgage rate cuts likely
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

UK mortgage borrowers could see further reductions in interest rates in the coming weeks, as lenders respond to favourable market conditions and heightened competition. With swap rates – the key funding costs for fixed-rate mortgages – continuing their downward trend, many financial institutions appear poised to trim their pricing.

Why mortgage rates may fall further

Several factors are contributing to the potential for additional rate reductions. Swap rates, which directly influence fixed mortgage pricing, have shown sustained declines in recent weeks. This movement typically precedes lenders adjusting their product offerings, particularly when combined with strong competition in the mortgage market.

Industry observers note that lenders are actively vying for business during what has become an increasingly competitive period. Many providers have already introduced rate cuts across various mortgage products, from two-year fixes to longer-term deals. This trend may accelerate if swap rates maintain their current trajectory.

What this means for borrowers

For those considering a new mortgage or remortgaging, the current climate presents potential opportunities. Homeowners approaching the end of their fixed terms might find it worthwhile to monitor rate movements closely, as even small reductions can translate into significant savings over the lifetime of a loan.

However, borrowers should bear in mind that rate changes can be unpredictable. While the market appears to be moving in a favourable direction, external economic factors could influence lenders' pricing strategies. Those with mortgages coming up for renewal may wish to consult a whole-of-market broker to explore all available options.

As the summer progresses, all eyes will be on whether lenders continue their rate-cutting trend. With competition remaining fierce and funding costs favourable, the coming weeks could bring further positive news for mortgage seekers.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

UK mortgage borrowers could see further reductions in interest rates in the coming weeks, as lenders respond to favourable market conditions and heightened competition. With swap rates – the key funding costs for fixed-rate mortgages – continuing their downward trend, many financial institutions appear poised to trim their pricing.

Why mortgage rates may fall further

Several factors are contributing to the potential for additional rate reductions. Swap rates, which directly influence fixed mortgage pricing, have shown sustained declines in recent weeks. This movement typically precedes lenders adjusting their product offerings, particularly when combined with strong competition in the mortgage market.

Industry observers note that lenders are actively vying for business during what has become an increasingly competitive period. Many providers have already introduced rate cuts across various mortgage products, from two-year fixes to longer-term deals. This trend may accelerate if swap rates maintain their current trajectory.

What this means for borrowers

For those considering a new mortgage or remortgaging, the current climate presents potential opportunities. Homeowners approaching the end of their fixed terms might find it worthwhile to monitor rate movements closely, as even small reductions can translate into significant savings over the lifetime of a loan.

However, borrowers should bear in mind that rate changes can be unpredictable. While the market appears to be moving in a favourable direction, external economic factors could influence lenders' pricing strategies. Those with mortgages coming up for renewal may wish to consult a whole-of-market broker to explore all available options.

As the summer progresses, all eyes will be on whether lenders continue their rate-cutting trend. With competition remaining fierce and funding costs favourable, the coming weeks could bring further positive news for mortgage seekers.

item.title: Why Valouran’s Matt Robertson believes credibility is the developer’s greatest asset
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The Importance of Credibility in Property Development

In the competitive world of prime property development, industry leaders suggest that credibility often proves more valuable than short-term financial gains. Matthew Robertson, Chief Financial Officer at Valouran, recently highlighted how trust, heritage and consistent delivery form the bedrock of successful development projects.

For homeowners and investors considering property developments, Robertson's insights may offer valuable perspective when evaluating potential projects. Established developers with proven track records might provide greater reassurance than newer entrants promising quick returns.

Key Factors in Developer Selection

When assessing property developers, prospective buyers might wish to consider:

  • Track record: Completed projects and client testimonials can demonstrate a developer's capability
  • Financial stability: Strong balance sheets may indicate better project completion prospects
  • Industry reputation: Awards, partnerships and peer recognition often reflect credibility
  • Transparency: Clear communication about timelines, specifications and potential challenges

Robertson's comments suggest that these qualitative factors could be as important as the financial aspects when selecting development opportunities. For those considering property investments, it may be worth researching developers' histories alongside evaluating the properties themselves.

While newer developers can certainly deliver excellent projects, established firms with decades of experience might offer additional peace of mind through their proven ability to navigate complex planning processes, construction challenges and market fluctuations.

As with any significant financial decision, potential buyers may benefit from thorough research and professional advice before committing to property investments. The development sector's leading figures continue to emphasise that credibility, once earned, becomes a developer's most valuable asset in building long-term success.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The Importance of Credibility in Property Development

In the competitive world of prime property development, industry leaders suggest that credibility often proves more valuable than short-term financial gains. Matthew Robertson, Chief Financial Officer at Valouran, recently highlighted how trust, heritage and consistent delivery form the bedrock of successful development projects.

For homeowners and investors considering property developments, Robertson's insights may offer valuable perspective when evaluating potential projects. Established developers with proven track records might provide greater reassurance than newer entrants promising quick returns.

Key Factors in Developer Selection

When assessing property developers, prospective buyers might wish to consider:

  • Track record: Completed projects and client testimonials can demonstrate a developer's capability
  • Financial stability: Strong balance sheets may indicate better project completion prospects
  • Industry reputation: Awards, partnerships and peer recognition often reflect credibility
  • Transparency: Clear communication about timelines, specifications and potential challenges

Robertson's comments suggest that these qualitative factors could be as important as the financial aspects when selecting development opportunities. For those considering property investments, it may be worth researching developers' histories alongside evaluating the properties themselves.

While newer developers can certainly deliver excellent projects, established firms with decades of experience might offer additional peace of mind through their proven ability to navigate complex planning processes, construction challenges and market fluctuations.

As with any significant financial decision, potential buyers may benefit from thorough research and professional advice before committing to property investments. The development sector's leading figures continue to emphasise that credibility, once earned, becomes a developer's most valuable asset in building long-term success.

item.title: 9 in 10 investors spending more on sustainability
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

A growing number of property investors are prioritising sustainability, with nearly 90% increasing their spending on energy-efficient improvements, according to recent industry findings. This shift reflects rising tenant demand for greener homes and tighter environmental regulations affecting the UK property market.

Why Investors Are Focusing on Sustainability

The push towards eco-friendly properties comes as energy efficiency becomes a key factor for both renters and buyers. Homeowners may wish to note that properties with higher EPC ratings often command higher rental yields and attract longer-term tenancies. Key areas of investment include:

  • Upgrading insulation and double glazing
  • Installing smart heating controls
  • Adding renewable energy sources like solar panels
  • Improving water efficiency through low-flow fixtures

The Financial Benefits of Green Improvements

Whilst the initial costs of sustainable upgrades can be significant, borrowers might consider how these improvements could lead to long-term savings and potentially higher property values. Mortgage lenders are increasingly offering green products with preferential rates for energy-efficient homes, making sustainability-focused renovations financially appealing.

Landlords in particular may find that energy-saving enhancements help future-proof their portfolios against upcoming Minimum Energy Efficiency Standards (MEES) changes, whilst also reducing void periods between tenancies.

As the property market continues to evolve, sustainability appears set to remain a key consideration for both investors and homeowners alike. Those reviewing their mortgage options may find it worthwhile exploring how green home improvements could affect both their borrowing potential and property's long-term value.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

A growing number of property investors are prioritising sustainability, with nearly 90% increasing their spending on energy-efficient improvements, according to recent industry findings. This shift reflects rising tenant demand for greener homes and tighter environmental regulations affecting the UK property market.

Why Investors Are Focusing on Sustainability

The push towards eco-friendly properties comes as energy efficiency becomes a key factor for both renters and buyers. Homeowners may wish to note that properties with higher EPC ratings often command higher rental yields and attract longer-term tenancies. Key areas of investment include:

  • Upgrading insulation and double glazing
  • Installing smart heating controls
  • Adding renewable energy sources like solar panels
  • Improving water efficiency through low-flow fixtures

The Financial Benefits of Green Improvements

Whilst the initial costs of sustainable upgrades can be significant, borrowers might consider how these improvements could lead to long-term savings and potentially higher property values. Mortgage lenders are increasingly offering green products with preferential rates for energy-efficient homes, making sustainability-focused renovations financially appealing.

Landlords in particular may find that energy-saving enhancements help future-proof their portfolios against upcoming Minimum Energy Efficiency Standards (MEES) changes, whilst also reducing void periods between tenancies.

As the property market continues to evolve, sustainability appears set to remain a key consideration for both investors and homeowners alike. Those reviewing their mortgage options may find it worthwhile exploring how green home improvements could affect both their borrowing potential and property's long-term value.

item.title: EPC assessment pilot to help landlords
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Landlords looking to improve their property's energy efficiency may benefit from a new pilot scheme launched by The Mortgage Works. The buy-to-let lender is offering free Energy Performance Certificate (EPC) assessments alongside discounted finance options to help property investors meet upcoming regulatory requirements.

Support for landlords ahead of 2030 changes

The initiative comes as the private rental sector prepares for stricter energy efficiency standards expected by 2030. Currently, rental properties in England and Wales must meet at least an EPC rating of E, but future regulations may require a minimum C rating. The Mortgage Works' pilot scheme aims to:

  • Provide complimentary EPC assessments to help landlords understand their property's current rating
  • Offer discounted financing options for energy efficiency improvements
  • Support landlords in planning necessary upgrades well in advance of regulatory deadlines

Why energy efficiency matters for landlords

Improving a property's EPC rating could bring several potential benefits for landlords beyond simply meeting future regulations. Properties with better energy efficiency ratings might:

  • Attract tenants more easily, as energy-efficient homes typically have lower running costs
  • Command higher rental values in some markets
  • Maintain better long-term property values as efficiency standards tighten
  • Reduce void periods between tenancies

Whilst the pilot scheme currently has limited availability, its introduction signals growing industry recognition of the challenges landlords face in upgrading older properties. Property investors concerned about future EPC requirements might wish to explore available support schemes and begin planning any necessary improvements.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Landlords looking to improve their property's energy efficiency may benefit from a new pilot scheme launched by The Mortgage Works. The buy-to-let lender is offering free Energy Performance Certificate (EPC) assessments alongside discounted finance options to help property investors meet upcoming regulatory requirements.

Support for landlords ahead of 2030 changes

The initiative comes as the private rental sector prepares for stricter energy efficiency standards expected by 2030. Currently, rental properties in England and Wales must meet at least an EPC rating of E, but future regulations may require a minimum C rating. The Mortgage Works' pilot scheme aims to:

  • Provide complimentary EPC assessments to help landlords understand their property's current rating
  • Offer discounted financing options for energy efficiency improvements
  • Support landlords in planning necessary upgrades well in advance of regulatory deadlines

Why energy efficiency matters for landlords

Improving a property's EPC rating could bring several potential benefits for landlords beyond simply meeting future regulations. Properties with better energy efficiency ratings might:

  • Attract tenants more easily, as energy-efficient homes typically have lower running costs
  • Command higher rental values in some markets
  • Maintain better long-term property values as efficiency standards tighten
  • Reduce void periods between tenancies

Whilst the pilot scheme currently has limited availability, its introduction signals growing industry recognition of the challenges landlords face in upgrading older properties. Property investors concerned about future EPC requirements might wish to explore available support schemes and begin planning any necessary improvements.

item.title: From bridge to BTL: when to apply for the refinance (and how not to miss the window)
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Navigating the transition from a bridging loan to a buy-to-let (BTL) mortgage requires careful timing and preparation. Many property investors find the refinancing stage more challenging than securing the initial bridging finance, with common pitfalls including missed deadlines, incorrect documentation, and unnecessary interest costs. Understanding the refinancing process could help landlords secure favourable terms without costly delays.

Timing Your BTL Refinance Application

Applying for a buy-to-let mortgage too early or too late after securing a bridging loan may lead to complications. Industry experts suggest these considerations:

  • Early applications: Lenders typically require proof of rental income, which may not be established if the property is newly acquired or undergoing refurbishment. Submitting too soon could result in rejection.
  • Last-minute applications: Processing times for BTL mortgages often take 4-8 weeks. Leaving applications until the bridge term is nearly expired risks costly extensions.
  • Optimal window: Many advisers recommend beginning the refinance process 2-3 months before the bridging loan term ends, allowing time for valuations and underwriting.

Preparing for a Smooth Transition

To improve the chances of a successful refinance, landlords might consider:

  • Documentation: Having tenancy agreements, proof of rental income, and property valuation reports ready before applying.
  • Credit health: Ensuring personal credit scores remain strong throughout the bridge period, as lenders will reassess affordability.
  • Exit strategy: Some bridging lenders require evidence of a refinance plan at the outset – maintaining communication with both lenders may prevent surprises.

While bridging finance provides short-term flexibility, the refinance stage ultimately determines the long-term viability of a property investment. Seeking professional mortgage advice early in the process could help investors align their timelines with lender requirements and market conditions. With careful planning, the transition from bridge to BTL need not be a stressful experience.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Navigating the transition from a bridging loan to a buy-to-let (BTL) mortgage requires careful timing and preparation. Many property investors find the refinancing stage more challenging than securing the initial bridging finance, with common pitfalls including missed deadlines, incorrect documentation, and unnecessary interest costs. Understanding the refinancing process could help landlords secure favourable terms without costly delays.

Timing Your BTL Refinance Application

Applying for a buy-to-let mortgage too early or too late after securing a bridging loan may lead to complications. Industry experts suggest these considerations:

  • Early applications: Lenders typically require proof of rental income, which may not be established if the property is newly acquired or undergoing refurbishment. Submitting too soon could result in rejection.
  • Last-minute applications: Processing times for BTL mortgages often take 4-8 weeks. Leaving applications until the bridge term is nearly expired risks costly extensions.
  • Optimal window: Many advisers recommend beginning the refinance process 2-3 months before the bridging loan term ends, allowing time for valuations and underwriting.

Preparing for a Smooth Transition

To improve the chances of a successful refinance, landlords might consider:

  • Documentation: Having tenancy agreements, proof of rental income, and property valuation reports ready before applying.
  • Credit health: Ensuring personal credit scores remain strong throughout the bridge period, as lenders will reassess affordability.
  • Exit strategy: Some bridging lenders require evidence of a refinance plan at the outset – maintaining communication with both lenders may prevent surprises.

While bridging finance provides short-term flexibility, the refinance stage ultimately determines the long-term viability of a property investment. Seeking professional mortgage advice early in the process could help investors align their timelines with lender requirements and market conditions. With careful planning, the transition from bridge to BTL need not be a stressful experience.
